learning_objectives: Define HIE models and their role in the healthcare ecosystem
Trace the evolution of HIE from regional networks to TEFCA
Understand the value proposition of health data exchange
readings: Health Information Exchange: Navigating and Managing a Networked World Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Health Information Exchange enables patient data to follow patients across organizational boundaries. Without HIE, each provider sees only a fragment of the patient's health story.
key_concepts: HIE value: care coordination, reduced duplicative testing, emergency access, public health reporting
HIE models: centralized, federated, hybrid, consumer-mediated
Exchange types: directed (push), query-based (pull), consumer-mediated (patient-driven)
Evolution: regional HIEs, state-designated entities, Commonwell, Carequality, TEFCA
Business models: subscription, transaction-based, government-funded
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walkthrough of a query-based HIE exchange: ED physician queries the HIE for a patient's medication list and recent discharge summary from another hospital

review.value: Trusted Exchange Framework and Common Agreement: ONC's national approach to connecting disparate health information networks for universal interoperability
Provider queries the HIE network to find and retrieve records for a specific patient from other participating organizations

learning_objectives: Describe the standards stack enabling health information exchange
Implement FHIR-based data exchange between systems
Understand the role of implementation guides in standardizing exchange

lecture_notes.introduction: Interoperability requires agreed-upon standards at every layer: transport, content, vocabulary, and workflow. Understanding this standards stack is essential for building exchange capabilities.
key_concepts: Transport standards: Direct Messaging (SMTP-based), FHIR RESTful API, IHE profiles
Content standards: C-CDA (Consolidated Clinical Document Architecture), FHIR resources
Vocabulary standards: SNOMED CT, LOINC, RxNorm, ICD-10 for semantic interoperability
Implementation guides: US Core, Da Vinci, CARIN for specific use cases
Testing and certification: ONC criteria, IHE Connectathons
lecture_notes.demonstration: Retrieve a patient's Continuity of Care Document (C-CDA) via Direct Messaging, then retrieve the same data via FHIR API, comparing the two approaches

review.value: Consolidated Clinical Document Architecture: XML-based standard for clinical document exchange, used in Transitions of Care and patient summaries
The ability of systems to not just exchange data but interpret its meaning consistently, enabled by shared vocabulary standards

learning_objectives: Analyze the TEFCA architecture and governance structure
Understand Qualified Health Information Network (QHIN) requirements
Evaluate TEFCA's potential to achieve universal health data exchange

lecture_notes.introduction: TEFCA represents the most ambitious attempt to create a national health data exchange network. Understanding its architecture and governance is essential for the future of interoperability.
key_concepts: TEFCA architecture: RCE (Recognized Coordinating Entity), QHINs, participants, sub-participants
Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs): individual access, treatment, payment, public health, government
QHIN technical requirements: connectivity, security, identity proofing, audit
Carequality and Commonwell as foundational exchange networks joining TEFCA
Challenges: patient matching without national ID, consent management, data quality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map a cross-network data exchange scenario: patient visits Hospital A (Epic/Carequality QHIN) then ED at Hospital B (Cerner/Commonwell QHIN) - trace the data flow through TEFCA

review.value: Qualified Health Information Network: organizations that meet TEFCA's technical, security, and governance requirements to serve as network-to-network exchange points
The challenge of linking records for the same patient across organizations without a national patient identifier, using probabilistic matching algorithms

class_schedule.topic: Consent Management and Privacy in HIE
learning_objectives: Design consent management frameworks for health data exchange
Understand opt-in vs. opt-out consent models and their state variations
Implement granular consent using FHIR Consent resources

lecture_notes.introduction: Patient trust in health data exchange depends on robust consent management. The patchwork of state consent laws creates complex compliance challenges for HIE networks operating across state lines.
key_concepts: Consent models: opt-in (patient must affirmatively consent), opt-out (patient must actively refuse), no-consent (treatment exception)
State consent law variation: some states require opt-in, others allow opt-out, creating a patchwork
Granular consent: patient controls which data, to which providers, for which purposes
FHIR Consent resource: computable representation of patient consent directives
Sensitive data segmentation: 42 CFR Part 2, mental health, HIV per state law
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a FHIR Consent resource allowing a patient to share all records except substance use disorder treatment with their PCP but share everything with their addiction specialist
setup_requirements: FHIR test server with Consent resource support

review.value: Opt-in: data not shared unless patient consents (stronger privacy, lower participation). Opt-out: data shared unless patient refuses (higher participation, privacy concerns)
Technical ability to tag and selectively share or withhold specific categories of sensitive health data based on consent and regulatory requirements

class_schedule.topic: Patient Identity Management and Record Matching
learning_objectives: Evaluate patient matching algorithms and their accuracy
Understand the challenge of operating without a national patient identifier
Design strategies for improving match rates across organizations
readings: ONC Patient Matching Algorithm Challenge Results

lecture_notes.introduction: Patient matching is the Achilles' heel of health information exchange. If you cannot reliably identify the same patient across systems, you cannot safely exchange their data.
key_concepts: The national patient identifier problem: Congressional prohibition, ongoing debate
Deterministic matching: exact match on multiple fields (name, DOB, SSN fragment)
Probabilistic matching: weighted scoring of multiple attributes (Fellegi-Sunter model)
Referential matching: using external reference databases to improve match accuracy
Match rate metrics: true positive, false positive, false negative rates and their clinical impact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run patient matching scenarios using a test dataset with common challenges: name changes, typos, transposed digits, common names, and evaluate match accuracy
setup_requirements: Patient matching test dataset with known duplicates

review.value: Incorrectly linking two different patients' records - potentially dangerous as clinician sees wrong patient's data informing treatment decisions
Algorithm assigning weights to matching and non-matching data elements, calculating a composite score to determine if two records represent the same patient

class_schedule.topic: Payer-Provider Data Exchange and Prior Authorization
learning_objectives: Describe the CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule
Evaluate the Da Vinci Implementation Guides for payer use cases
readings: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule (CMS-0057) Summary

lecture_notes.introduction: Interoperability is expanding beyond provider-to-provider exchange to include payer-provider data sharing. CMS rules now require payers to expose FHIR APIs for member data access and streamline prior authorization.
key_concepts: CMS-0057 rule: Patient Access API, Provider Access API, Payer-to-Payer API requirements
Prior Authorization API: FHIR-based electronic prior auth reducing provider burden
Da Vinci project: HL7 implementation guides for payer use cases (Coverage Requirements, Prior Auth, Alerts)
Attachment exchange: FHIR-based clinical data exchange for claims adjudication
Impact on provider workflow: reducing fax-based prior authorization from days to minutes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a FHIR-based prior authorization workflow using Da Vinci CRD (Coverage Requirements Discovery) and DTR (Documentation Templates and Rules)
setup_requirements: FHIR test server with payer resources

review.value: CMS rule requiring Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, and CHIP plans to implement FHIR APIs for patient data access, provider data sharing, and electronic prior authorization
Coverage Requirements Discovery: FHIR-based CDS Hook that queries the payer at the point of ordering to determine coverage and prior auth requirements in real time

questions.question: Compare the three main HIE exchange types (directed, query-based, and consumer-mediated) and provide a clinical scenario where each is most appropriate.
questions.answer: Directed exchange (push): secure point-to-point transmission of clinical documents between known parties. Best for: a PCP sending a referral summary to a specialist for a scheduled appointment - both parties are known and the content is predetermined. Query-based exchange (pull): provider queries the HIE network to find and retrieve available records for a patient. Best for: an ED physician treating an unconscious patient from another state who needs medication history and allergies - the provider doesn't know who has the records and needs to search broadly. Consumer-mediated exchange: patient controls the flow of their health data between providers. Best for: a patient consolidating records from multiple specialists into a personal health record app, then sharing selected information with a new provider when relocating.

rubric.key: All three exchange types correctly described
Clear rationale for why each scenario fits the exchange type

questions.question: Explain the TEFCA architecture, including the roles of the RCE, QHINs, Participants, and Sub-Participants. Why is this layered approach necessary?
questions.answer: TEFCA architecture layers: RCE (Recognized Coordinating Entity - currently the Sequoia Project): manages the Common Agreement, certifies QHINs, resolves disputes, and evolves the framework. QHINs (Qualified Health Information Networks - e.g., Carequality, Commonwell, eHealth Exchange): large networks that connect directly to each other, meeting stringent technical and security requirements. They serve as the backbone. Participants: organizations that connect through a QHIN (e.g., a hospital system connecting via a QHIN to exchange data nationally). Sub-Participants: smaller organizations that connect through a Participant (e.g., a small clinic connecting through its hospital system's network). The layered approach is necessary because: it is impractical for every provider to connect to every other provider directly. QHINs provide scalable network-of-networks architecture. Each layer has appropriate requirements scaled to its role. It builds on existing infrastructure rather than replacing it.

rubric.key: Valid rationale for the layered approach

questions.question: Explain why patient matching is considered the greatest challenge in health information exchange. Describe the clinical risks of both false positive and false negative matches.
questions.answer: Patient matching is the greatest HIE challenge because the U.S. lacks a national patient identifier, forcing reliance on demographic data (name, DOB, address, SSN fragment) that varies in quality across systems. Common challenges include name changes (marriage), data entry errors, common names, and homeless patients without stable addresses. False positive risk (matching two different patients): A clinician sees another patient's allergies, medications, or diagnoses. This could lead to wrong treatment decisions - for example, withholding a needed medication due to a false allergy, or administering a contraindicated drug based on incorrect medication history. False negative risk (failing to match the same patient): The clinician does not see the patient's existing records, missing critical information. This leads to duplicative testing, missed drug interactions, repeated imaging with unnecessary radiation exposure, and care decisions without complete clinical context.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of why matching is difficult
Clinical risks of false positives with examples
Clinical risks of false negatives with examples

questions.question: A patient in a state with an opt-in consent law wants to participate in HIE but exclude her substance use disorder treatment records. Describe the technical and policy mechanisms needed to honor this granular consent.
questions.answer: Policy mechanisms: (1) The HIE must support granular consent that allows sharing most records while excluding specific categories. (2) The consent form must clearly communicate what will and will not be shared. (3) The consent must comply with both state opt-in requirements and 42 CFR Part 2 protections for SUD records. Technical mechanisms: (1) Data segmentation: SUD-related records must be tagged with sensitivity codes (e.g., DS4P - Data Segmentation for Privacy) in the source EHR. (2) FHIR Consent resource: a computable consent directive specifying permitted and restricted data categories, linked to the patient's record in the HIE. (3) Access control enforcement: the HIE query engine must evaluate the consent directive at query time, filtering SUD-tagged records from responses to non-authorized requestors. (4) Audit logging: all consent decisions and data filtering events must be logged for compliance verification.

rubric.key: Policy mechanisms including 42 CFR Part 2 compliance
Technical mechanisms for data segmentation and filtering

major_assignments.title: Health Information Exchange Strategy and Implementation Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive HIE strategy for a multi-hospital health system joining TEFCA, including technical architecture, consent management, patient matching strategy, and phased implementation plan.
objectives: Design an HIE architecture aligned with TEFCA requirements
Address patient matching and consent management challenges
Evaluate costs, benefits, and risks of HIE participation
deliverables: Phased implementation roadmap with budget estimates

criteria: Use cases prioritized with clinical justification
Patient matching strategy with accuracy targets
Robust technical architecture with standards compliance
Thoughtful consent management and patient matching approach
Realistic implementation plan with clear success metrics

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of HIE concepts, standards, and interoperability knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on labs with FHIR APIs, C-CDA documents, and consent management

major_assignments.description: HIE strategy and implementation plan project

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of HIE models, standards, and TEFCA

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including consent, matching, and payer interoperability

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Lab sessions are mandatory. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day up to 5 days.
course_policies.collaboration: Labs may be done in pairs. Strategy project and written work must be individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Standards specifications may be referenced but analysis must be the student's own.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with FHIR client tools (Postman), internet access, C-CDA viewer software.
